title: REGIONAL GUIDELINES FOR CODEX CONTACT POINTS AND NATIONAL CODEX COMMITTEES (NEAR EAST)

sections.section: REGIONAL GUIDELINES FOR CODEX CONTACT POINTS AND NATIONAL CODEX COMMITTEES (NEAR EAST)
sections.summary: Document title/heading: Regional Guidelines for Codex Contact Points and National Codex Committees (Near East); this is a scope heading rather than substantive provisions.
Document identifier: CAC/GL 58R-2005; this is a document code heading and not substantive text.

Introduces the guidelines by stating that Codex Contact Points and National Codex Committees are essential for stakeholder involvement and effective national participation in Codex, and that the document outlines basic guidelines for their establishment, structure, tasks and organisation to improve efficiency.
Section heading only: 'Codex Contact Point'—no substantive provisions in this entry.

Recommends establishment of a Codex Contact Point in every member country and defines its function as the official national link/communication agent between the Codex Alimentarius Commission and the member country, handling all communications to and from the Commission and within national authorities.

Lists the tasks of the national Codex Contact Point, including receiving all Codex communications and publications, initiating action on received documents (directly or via the National Codex Committee/sub‑committees), maintaining a library of Codex texts, distributing documents to stakeholders, acting as the link between the Codex Secretariat and the member country, and performing other tasks recommended by the National Codex Committee or government.

Decision on the location of the national Codex Contact Point rests with the member country, may be made by the government or via consultation with relevant national stakeholders, and it is common practice for the government to assume hosting responsibility.

sections.section: 9. Whatever the location of the national Codex Contact Point, it is desirable that the location meets the following criteria:
sections.summary: Specifies desirable location criteria for the national Codex Contact Point: it should be as neutral as possible regarding interested parties, capable of performing Codex Contact Point tasks, and accessible to all parties involved in Codex activities.

Lists basic requirements for a Codex Contact Point, including committed staff, a code of conduct, adequate office space, communication facilities (telephone, fax, e‑mail, postal address), stationery, and printing/photocopying equipment.

sections.section: 10. The basic requirements for a Codex Contact Point may include the following:
sections.summary: Outlines that the basic requirements for a Codex Contact Point may include committed staff, a code of conduct, adequate office space, communications (telephone, fax, e‑mail, postal address), stationery, and printing/photocopying equipment.

Defines the National Codex Committee as a consultative body to the government on Codex matters and states that representatives from all relevant national bodies (e.g., consumer organizations, professional associations) should be invited to contribute views to the national negotiating position.

sections.section: 11. The primary objective of the National Codex Committee is to act as a consultative group to the government on matters related to Codex.
sections.summary: States that the primary objective of the National Codex Committee is to act as a consultative group to the government on matters related to Codex.

sections.section: 12. Representatives from all relevant national bodies, including consumers' organizations, professional associations, etc. should be invited to attend meetings of the National Codex Committee to put forward their views for consideration and inclusion in the national negotiating position.
sections.summary: Requires that representatives from all relevant national bodies, including consumer organizations and professional associations, be invited to National Codex Committee meetings to present views for consideration and inclusion in the national negotiating position.

Enumerates the National Codex Committee's primary tasks: advising the government on Codex standards and implementation; formulating the country's response to Codex Alimentarius Commission proposals; proposing representatives for Codex meetings; appointing technical subcommittees as needed; and performing other duties as the government deems appropriate.

sections.section: 13. The primary tasks of the National Codex Committee may include:
sections.summary: Lists the primary tasks of the National Codex Committee: advising the government on Codex standards and their implementation; formulating the country's response to Codex Alimentarius Commission proposals; proposing national representatives for Codex meetings; appointing technical subcommittees as necessary; and carrying out other duties as the government deems appropriate.

Provides an example composition and structure for a National Codex Committee, recommending representatives from relevant ministries and government institutions (e.g., health, agriculture, trade, environment, national standards, government laboratories), local authorities, consumer organizations, professional associations, universities, research organizations and notable experts.

sections.section: 14. An example of a National Codex Committee could include representatives from the following institutions:
sections.summary: Gives an example list of institutions to be represented on a National Codex Committee, including relevant ministries (health, agriculture, trade, environment), national standards bodies and laboratories, local authorities, consumer groups, industry associations, universities, research organizations and experts.

sections.section: NOMINATION TO THE NATIONAL CODEX COMMITTEE
sections.summary: Requires that nomination to the National Codex Committee be known, open and transparent (with countries free to use different selection methods), and mandates an annual review of participation with new members appointed to replace inactive members.

States that the source of funding for the Codex Contact Point and National Codex Committee must be known to all members and that contributions must be unconditional to ensure impartiality and avoid conflicts of interest.
